context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: New Institutionalism and Law

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the basis for the belief that law will lead to development? Is law a tool for development or is a development goal in itself? What is the meaning of institutions – can one unbundle them and if so, what is the role of law as an institution as opposed to other institutions, in development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m stuck on this reflection abt law + development, and idk what the prompt is really asking. can u just explain the whole thing in a simple way first?
exchanges.tutor:
I get that it feels like a lot at once! But let's work through it together rather than me breaking it down for you.

Look at the very first question in the prompt: 'What is the basis for the belief that law will lead to development?' 

Before we go anywhere else — what do YOU think that question is asking? Just take a shot at it in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole prompt for them, which would essentially do the intellectual work of unpacking the assignment for them. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to help them engage with the prompt themselves. I'll acknowledge their confusion but turn it back to them with a guiding question that helps them identify what they already understand, starting with the most basic element of the prompt.

exchanges.student: ngl idk, it feels like it’s asking literally why ppl think laws make countries richer or more “developed”? That prob sounds too basic tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually not basic at all — that's a solid starting intuition. So now push it a little further: WHY would someone believe that? What would law need to DO in a society for it to lead to development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually grasped the core intuition behind the first question quite well. I should validate this without over-praising, and push them to go a bit deeper with a follow-up question that moves toward the substance of the argument. I want to avoid giving them the answer but help them build on what they already know.

exchanges.student: maybe bc law makes things more stable/predictable for business and govt stuff? tbh i’m not sure if they want econ reasons, political reasons, or like colonial history too.
exchanges.tutor: Stability and predictability for business — that's a meaningful idea. What kind of business behavior does that actually enable? Think concretely: what can a business DO when there's a stable legal framework that it can't do without one?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress - their answer about stability and predictability is genuinely on the right track and touches on key institutionalist ideas. However, they're now getting a bit anxious about covering all angles. Rather than reassuring them by listing what to cover, I should affirm their current thread and push them to develop it further before jumping to other dimensions. This keeps the conversation focused and productive.

exchanges.student: i guess invest more, sign contracts, trust property rights, hire ppl? also that angle worked kinda too well lol, so maybe i’m still confused abt the institutions part rn.
exchanges.tutor: Good list — you're building something real there. Now on institutions: before worrying about 'unbundling' them, what do you think an institution even IS? Don't look it up — just take a guess based on how you'd use the word.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made great progress on the first question and is now naturally transitioning to the institutions question, which is good. I should affirm their answers briefly and redirect them to think about the institutions question themselves rather than explaining it to them. The prompt asks about 'unbundling' institutions, which is a specific and important concept. I'll nudge them toward thinking about what an institution even is before getting to unbundling.

exchanges.student: idk, like rules + orgs + norms all mixed together? i’m kinda tempted to just get a big overview first bc rn it feels scattered.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely good definition — rules, orgs, and norms. Now here's the thing: if institutions are all of those things mixed together, what might it mean to 'unbundle' them? Why would someone even want to separate them out?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's definition of institutions is actually quite good and close to how the term is used in new institutionalism. I should affirm this briefly. However, the student is now asking for a big overview, which would be me doing the work for them. I should resist this and instead use their own definition to push them toward the 'unbundling' question, which is the next logical step. Their definition already sets up the unbundling question naturally.

exchanges.student: maybe to see which part actually matters? like law vs informal norms vs state capacity? tbh can u just tell me if law is supposed to be the main institution here or not.
exchanges.tutor: I won't tell you that — but notice you basically just answered your own question. You said 'law vs informal norms vs state capacity' — so what's DISTINCTIVE about law compared to those other two? What can law do that an informal norm can't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the answer directly, which I should refuse. However, their intuition about separating out which part matters is excellent and directly relevant to the assignment. I should acknowledge that and turn it back into a question that helps them reason through law's specific role themselves. This is a key moment - their unbundling intuition is strong and I want to build on it.

exchanges.student: maybe law is formal and enforceable by the state, but ngl i’m losing the thread. that didn’t work, trying diff angle: can u just show me a rough thesis i could react to?
exchanges.tutor: I can't write a thesis for you — that's your job, not mine. But honestly? Look at what you've already figured out across this conversation: law enables investment and contracts, institutions can be unbundled, and law is distinctive because it's formal and state-enforceable. You have the building blocks. Try writing one sentence that connects those ideas — what is law's specific role in development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write content for them, which I must refuse clearly - this falls under the rule of not editing, polishing, revising, writing, or suggesting language. However, their answer about law being formal and state-enforceable is actually excellent and directly relevant. I should be firm about not writing for them, affirm their progress, and redirect them to use what they've built so far. We're also getting close to the point where I should ask them to start integrating their ideas into a written attempt.

exchanges.student: maybe: law can support development by making rules enforceable and predictable, but it’s only one part of broader institutions, so it’s more a tool than the whole goal?
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid working thesis. But notice the prompt specifically asks: is law a tool FOR development, or a goal IN ITSELF? You leaned toward 'tool' — but can you think of any argument for why someone might say law is a development goal on its own terms, not just a means to an end?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely good working thesis that addresses multiple parts of the prompt. I should affirm this clearly without over-praising, and now push them to address the part of the prompt they haven't tackled yet: whether law is a tool FOR development or a development GOAL IN ITSELF. Their sentence leans toward 'tool' but they haven't really wrestled with the other side of that question yet, which is important for a reflection piece.

exchanges.student: maybe bc rule of law matters for justice/rights even if growth doesn’t happen? i’m still fuzzy tho, like do i need to argue both sides equally or pick one hard.
exchanges.tutor: Good insight on rights and justice — that's exactly the tension the prompt wants you to explore. On your structure question: it's a reflection piece, so you should take a position, but engage with the complexity. You now have enough to start writing. What's stopping you from putting a first draft together?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just hit on an important insight - law as intrinsically valuable for rights and justice, not just instrumentally for growth. This is excellent. Their question about structure (argue both sides vs. pick one) is a legitimate assignment comprehension question I can briefly address, but I shouldn't over-explain. We're also at exchange 9 of 10, so I should push them toward integrating everything into their reflection piece.

exchanges.student: honestly i freeze at intros lol, can u give me just a super generic opening line i can build from?
exchanges.tutor: I can't write any part of it for you — even one line. But here's a trick: don't start with an intro. Start by writing the idea you feel most confident about from our conversation. What's the one thing you're most sure of after everything we've discussed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write for them, which I must firmly refuse. This is a clear boundary. However, freezing at introductions is a real and common problem, so I should acknowledge it but redirect them using a Socratic approach. Instead of writing anything for them, I can ask them a question that helps them think about how to start without me generating language. We're at the last exchange so I should also summarize their progress and encourage them to just start writing.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. Every time the student asked for direct answers, thesis examples, or opening lines, the tutor declined and redirected back to the student's own reasoning. The tutor consistently built on the student's prior contributions, advanced the dialogue progressively, and maintained a warm, encouraging tone without being evaluative. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never revealed conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. The student generated all key ideas (stability/predictability, unbundling, law as formal/enforceable, tool vs. goal tension) through guided questioning. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on each student contribution (e.g., 'That's a solid working thesis,' then pushing further on the tool/goal distinction), and used diagnostic questions before offering any framing. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback, notably in Turn 7 summarizing what the student had built and directing them to synthesize it into a thesis sentence. 2.1–2.2: The dialogue progressed without repetitive cycling; the tutor kept the student anchored to the assignment even when the student tried to drift toward shortcuts. 3.1–3.2: Responses were concise and interactive, tone was supportive and non-evaluative throughout, and no grading language was used.
